text:
Before high-level soccer became a globalized commodity, the FIFA World Cup offered fans around the globe a rare chance to watch so many of the world’s greats. Before widespread international travel and worldwide TV streaming was the norm, players like Eusebio for Benfica in Portugal, Pele for Santos in Brazil, and Franz Beckenbauer for Bayern Munich in Germany played most or all of their careers with clubs in their home countries. As the game grew more commercialized and globalized, star players (and their fans) benefitted by taking advantage of opportunities abroad. Names recognized across borders and generations, from Johann Cruyff to Diego Maradona to Ronaldinho, became household names playing abroad while returning home for national team play.

Today we see a mixture of approaches, with many players playing most or all of their careers outside their home countries while others stay local for both club and country. Predictably, it’s easier for the stars of England, Spain and Germany to play their club football domestically, but increased investment in leagues like MLS in the United States and the Saudi Pro League have made those countries more popular as well, for both local and international players.

But this globalization has not been equally distributed. Even as individual African players and successful teams have had moments in the global spotlight, the clubs of the continent have remained largely anonymous on the game’s biggest stage. In the last men’s World Cup in 2022, just 2% of the tournament’s players were called in from African clubs — even though African nations made up 16% of the tournament as a whole. In this piece we’ll explore what has shifted and what hasn’t as Africa’s footballers have sought to make a living elsewhere.

At the 1994 World Cup — the last time the tournament was played in the United States — the world's national squads were broadly self-contained. CONCACAF sides drew 80% of players from domestic leagues; Asian confederations, nearly 96%. Even South America still fielded sides where 62% of players earned wages at home.

title: Nigeria, 1994
text: Nigeria arrived at USA '94 without a single player from what was then called the Nigeria Premier League; all 22 of their players in that tournament earned wages in Europe, illustrating how little the Super Eagles’ identity was tied to the domestic league.

title: Tunisia, 1998
text: Four years later, Tunisia offered a counter-example. Eighteen of their 23 players were at domestic clubs — the highest retention rate any African side would ever record in the modern day at a World Cup.

title: South Africa, 2010
text: South Africa in 2010 were the hosts, and their squad showed it. Sixteen of 23 players came from the South African Premiership — the only time a CAF side has been majority-domestic in the 21st century. State investment, local pride, and hosting rights conspired to produce something anomalous.

title: Nigeria, 2010
text: Nigeria were at the same tournament, again with zero players from the Nigeria Premier League; their flows point entirely to Europe. Similarly, Cameroon and Cote d’Ivoire competed with just one player apiece who represented the top flight in their respective home countries.

title: Brazil, 2014
text: By 2014, the export of African talent to European leagues was effectively total. While the CONMEBOL nations of South America have shown similar patterns of exporting many of the world’s best footballing talent overseas, continental powers like Atletico Minero in Brazil, Boca Juniors in Argentina, and Universidad de Chile at least saw some of their active current players on the pitch at the Brazil-hosted tournament.

title: Ghana, 2014
text: Meanwhile, the CAF domestic rate fell below 10% for the first time. Ghana and Cote d’Ivoire each only had one player from a domestic club, while Algeria and Cameroon only had two apiece.

title: Morocco, 2022
text: Morocco reached the World Cup semifinal in 2022 — the best result by an African team in history. Three of their 26 players were at domestic clubs. The rest played in Europe or the Gulf.

As squads for 2026 begin to trickle out, the expanded field could represent new opportunities for the African game. The expanded field means the number of teams representing CAF has doubled for this tournament. While the traditional powers of club football are always well-represented, other clubs from Paris Saint-Germain to Saprissa to Inter Miami have seen their brands grow around the world thanks to their players starring for their national teams in the World Cup. Will this be the year that one of Africa’s clubs joins the list?
